mcqs.question: What type of interface is RandomAccess in Java?

options.text: functional interface with one abstract method
An abstract class with concrete methods

mcqs.explanation: RandomAccess is a marker interface, meaning it has no methods but serves as a tag to indicate that a List implementation supports fast random access.

mcqs.question: Which of these List implementations implements RandomAccess?
mcqs.explanation: ArrayList implements RandomAccess because it uses an array internally, allowing O(1) access time. LinkedList does not implement it due to O(n) access costs.

mcqs.question: What performance benefit does RandomAccess provide?

options.text: Enables O(1) random access to elements
Reduces memory usage of the List

mcqs.explanation: RandomAccess signals that the List implementation supports O(1) random access, allowing algorithms to use index-based operations efficiently.

mcqs.question: In the binarySearch method, what happens if the List implements RandomAccess?

options.text: Converts the List to an ArrayList

mcqs.explanation: If the List implements RandomAccess, the method uses indexedBinarySearch, which is optimized for O(1) access. Otherwise, it falls back to iteratorBinarySearch for non-RandomAccess lists like LinkedList.

mcqs.question: Why is LinkedList not a RandomAccess implementation?

options.text: Because it uses a linked node structure with O(n) access time
Because it is not part of the java.util package
Because it is thread-safe by default
Because it does not implement the List interface

mcqs.explanation: LinkedList uses a doubly-linked node structure, which requires traversing nodes to access elements, resulting in O(n) access time. This makes it inefficient for random access operations.
